papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great progress in responding to user questions, allowing for a multitude of diverse applications. Yet, the quality of LLM outputs heavily depends on the prompt design, where a good prompt might enable the LLM to answer a very challenging question correctly. Therefore, recent works have developed many strategies for improving the prompt, including both manual crafting and in-domain optimization. However, their efficacy in unrestricted scenarios remains questionable, as the former depends on human design for specific questions and the latter usually generalizes poorly to unseen scenarios. To address these problems, we give LLMs the freedom to design the best prompts according to themselves. Specifically, we include a hierarchy of LLMs, first constructing a prompt with precise instructions and accurate wording in a hierarchical manner, and then using this prompt to generate the final answer to the user query. We term this pipeline Hierarchical Multi-Agent Workflow, or HMAW. In contrast with prior works, HMAW imposes no human restriction and requires no training, and is completely task-agnostic while capable of adjusting to the nuances of the underlying task. Through both quantitative and qualitative experiments across multiple benchmarks, we verify that despite its simplicity, the proposed approach can create detailed and suitable prompts, further boosting the performance of current LLMs.
papers.title: Towards Hierarchical Multi-Agent Workflows for Zero-Shot Prompt Optimization
papers.tldr: This work includes a hierarchy of LLMs, first constructing a prompt with precise instructions and accurate wording in a hierarchical manner, and then using this prompt to generate the final answer to the user query, and term this pipeline Hierarchical Multi-Agent Workflow, or HMAW.

papers.abstract: Using persona-conditioned LLMs as synthetic survey respondents has become a common practice in computational social science and agent-based simulations. Yet, it remains unclear whether multi-attribute persona prompting improves LLM reliability or instead introduces distortions. Here we contribute to this assessment by leveraging a large dataset of U.S. microdata from the World Values Survey. Concretely, we evaluate two open-weight chat models and a random-guesser baseline across more than 70K respondent-item instances. We find that persona prompting does not yield a clear aggregate improvement in survey alignment and, in many cases, significantly degrades performance. Persona effects are highly heterogeneous as most items exhibit minimal change, while a small subset of questions and underrepresented subgroups experience disproportionate distortions. Our findings highlight a key adverse impact of current persona-based simulation practices: demographic conditioning can redistribute error in ways that undermine subgroup fidelity and risk misleading downstream analyses.
papers.title: Assessing the Reliability of Persona-Conditioned LLMs as Synthetic Survey Respondents
papers.tldr: It is found that persona prompting does not yield a clear aggregate improvement in survey alignment and, in many cases, significantly degrades performance, highlighting a key adverse impact of current persona-based simulation practices.
papers.venue: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2026

papers.abstract: Background/Objectives: Artificial intelligence (AI) is transforming higher education (HE), reshaping teaching, learning, and feedback processes. Feedback generated by large language models (LLMs) has shown potential for enhancing student learning outcomes. However, few empirical studies have directly compared the quality of LLM feedback with feedback from novices and experts. This study investigates (1) the types of prompts needed to ensure high-quality LLM feedback in teacher education and (2) how feedback from novices, experts, and LLMs compares in terms of quality. Methods: To address these questions, we developed a theory-driven manual to evaluate prompt quality and designed three prompts of varying quality. Feedback generated by ChatGPT-4 was assessed alongside feedback from novices and experts, who were provided with the highest-quality prompt. Results: Our findings reveal that only the best prompt consistently produced high-quality feedback. Additionally, LLM feedback outperformed novice feedback and, in the categories explanation, questions, and specificity, even surpassed expert feedback in quality while being generated more quickly. Conclusions: These results suggest that LLMs, when guided by well-crafted prompts, can serve as high-quality and efficient alternatives to expert feedback. The findings underscore the importance of prompt quality and emphasize the need for prompt design guidelines to maximize the potential of LLMs in teacher education.
papers.title: The Promises and Pitfalls of Large Language Models as Feedback Providers: A Study of Prompt Engineering and the Quality of AI-Driven Feedback
papers.tldr: LLMs, when guided by well-crafted prompts, can serve as high-quality and efficient alternatives to expert feedback, and underscore the importance of prompt quality.

papers.abstract: Creativity relates to the ability to generate novel and effective ideas in the areas of interest. How are such creative ideas generated? One possible mechanism that supports creative ideation and is gaining increased empirical attention is by asking questions. Question asking is a likely cognitive mechanism that allows defining problems, facilitating creative problem solving. However, much is unknown about the exact role of questions in creativity. This work presents an attempt to apply text mining methods to measure the cognitive potential of questions, taking into account, among others, (a) question type, (b) question complexity, and (c) the content of the answer. This contribution summarizes the history of question mining as a part of creativity research, along with the natural language processing methods deemed useful or helpful in the study. In addition, a novel approach is proposed, implemented, and applied to five datasets. The experimental results obtained are comprehensively analyzed, suggesting that natural language processing has a role to play in creative research.

It draws heavily on evaluation frameworks from medicine, computational biology, and software engineering to measure the utility of LLM-driven scientific innovation.
persona_pool.framing: The transition from general-purpose assistants to specialized multi-agent roles enhances the diversity and validity of scientific hypotheses by simulating institutional oversight and structured collaboration.
instructions: Prioritize evidence that compares multi-agent interaction or 'uncertainty injection' against baseline zero-shot prompting.
Evaluate methodological rigor based on the agent's ability to simulate peer-review standards and institutional constraints.
Discount claims of novelty that lack validation against literature unseen during the model's training period.
Focus on the feasibility of hypotheses by assessing the model's alignment with human scientific goals and ethical safety boundaries.
Predict that role-based specialization will outperform general assistants specifically in tasks requiring cross-disciplinary synthesis.
persona_pool.methods_summary: Benchmarks of zero-shot and multi-agent hypothesis generation; Artificial Intelligence Structured Clinical Examinations (AI-SCE); comparative evaluations of LLM-as-reviewer vs. human-in-the-loop frameworks; cross-disciplinary surveys of AI-assisted discovery; and multi-role taxonomies (Assistant, Collaborator, Scientist, Evaluator).
persona_pool.name: Computer Science · AI-Augmented Discovery

persona_pool.background: This community focuses on the transition from AI as a tool to autonomous scientific agents that integrate structured memory and self-assessment. Its evidence base is built on multi-level taxonomies of AI autonomy and performance benchmarks for specialized agent swarms across interdisciplinary discovery tasks.
persona_pool.framing: Role-based multi-agent architectures enhance research hypothesis quality by simulating specialized collaborative structures that mitigate the knowledge gaps and 'ill-formed' reasoning found in single general-purpose models.
instructions: Prioritize evidence from multi-agent systems that include a dedicated 'critic' or 'self-assessment' module over those that rely on a single model output.
Evaluate hypothesis quality based on the agent's ability to integrate domain-specific constraints and biological or physical principles rather than general linguistic fluency.
Assess methodological rigor by the transparency of the agentic workflow and its alignment with established iterative discovery frameworks like EXHYTE.
Give higher weight to studies that demonstrate measurable improvements in accuracy or error reduction through collaborative role-assignment structures.
Predict that specialized role-based agents will consistently outperform general-purpose models in complex, multi-step scientific reasoning tasks.
persona_pool.methods_summary: Systematic surveys of agentic architectures, multi-agent frameworks, and collaborative graph-based reasoning models; benchmarking of large language models (LLMs) across scientific discovery cycles (e.g., EXHYTE); design and testing of critic modules and autonomous 'lab-pilot' systems in biomedical and physical domains.

persona_pool.framing: Role-based agent architectures improve research hypothesis generation by simulating domain-specific constraints and collaborative task orchestration that a general-purpose model lacks.

Discount claims of novelty that do not account for the influence of prompt manipulation or systematic biases inherent in role-based architectures.

persona_pool.methods_summary: Systematic surveys, benchmark taxonomies, and design frameworks for LLM-based agents; evaluation of multi-agent system (MAS) orchestration, role-playing agent (RPA) design, and human-agent interaction (HAI) systems; analysis of integrity attacks and safeguarding mechanisms.
persona_pool.name: Computer Science · Agentic Orchestration

persona_pool.background: This community draws from HCI, CSCW, and participatory design to evaluate how AI tools integrate into human workflows. persona_pool.framing: Role-based LLM agents improve research hypothesis novelty and methodological rigor by simulating diverse stakeholder perspectives, though they risk reducing idea diversity and user ownership compared to open-ended assistant models.
instructions: Prioritize findings from empirical user studies or participatory designs over purely technical benchmarks.
Evaluate the 'novelty' of hypotheses by looking for evidence of creative homogeneity and the potential for model-led modes to narrow the solution space.

Weight feasibility according to how well the AI-generated protocols or interview questions align with actual practitioner constraints and ethical standards.
Distinguish between perceived utility by the user and objective measures of hypothesis quality or diversity.

instructions: Prioritize evidence that measures the internal consistency and temporal stability of a persona over one-off performance metrics.

Evaluate whether the role-based agent demonstrates a distinct cognitive profile (e.g., specific personality traits) that justifies its use over a general assistant.
Treat the failure to replicate human-like behavioral distributions in strategic games as a major indicator of low simulation fidelity.
Distinguish between a model's ability to 'report' a persona's views and its ability to 'apply' that persona's logic to complex tasks.

role_personas.name: Methods Critic
role_personas.stance: Looks for measurable constructs, confounds, and viable comparisons.

role_personas.name: Literature Scout
role_personas.stance: Connects the idea to adjacent papers and unresolved gaps.

role_personas.name: Novelty Reviewer
role_personas.stance: Pushes the idea toward a sharper, less obvious research claim.
